title: Kāmasūtra
author: Vātsyāyana (Mallanāga)
genre: sūtra / śāstra (treatise on erotics and the art of living)
date_estimate: c. 3rd century CE (range 2nd-4th c. CE; final form 3rd-5th c. CE)
summary: The foundational Sanskrit treatise on kāma (pleasure, desire, eros), covering courtship, marriage, sexual technique, the conduct of the urbane man-about-town (nāgaraka), the lives of courtesans, and the use of aphrodisiacs and intrigues. Organized into seven books, it presents an analytical, worldly account of erotic and social life rather than a mere manual, situating kāma within the three aims of human life alongside dharma and artha.
history: Attributed to Vātsyāyana, whose name survives in the colophon though almost nothing else is known of him; he states he is condensing earlier (now lost) works on the subject. Composed probably in northern India (perhaps near Pāṭaliputra), it became the standard authority on kāmaśāstra and attracted commentaries, most famously the Jayamaṅgalā of Yaśodhara (13th c.).

title: Carakasaṃhitā
author: Agniveśa (original); redacted by Caraka and later by Dṛḍhabala
date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE (Caraka redaction); Dṛḍhabala's final redaction c. 4th c. CE; disputed
summary: One of the two foundational classics of Āyurveda, dealing chiefly with internal medicine (kāyacikitsā). It systematizes the theory of the three doṣas, diagnosis, pathology, pharmacology, dietetics, ethics of the physician, and the philosophy of life and health, organized into eight sthānas (sections).
history: Traditionally derived from the teachings of Ātreya Punarvasu, compiled by his pupil Agniveśa as the Agniveśa-tantra, then redacted by Caraka and partly lost; the missing portions were supplied by Dṛḍhabala (c. 4th c. CE). Its authoritative medieval commentary is the Āyurvedadīpikā of Cakrapāṇidatta (11th c.). It remains a cornerstone of classical Indian medicine.

title: Suśrutasaṃhitā
author: Suśruta (with later redactors)
date_estimate: core perhaps mid-1st millennium BCE; text as preserved c. 3rd-4th c. CE; disputed
summary: The classic Āyurvedic treatise specializing in śalyatantra (surgery), describing surgical instruments, procedures (including plastic and cataract surgery), wound management, anatomy via dissection, toxicology, and obstetrics, alongside general medicine. It transmits the teachings ascribed to the sage Divodāsa Dhanvantari of Kāśī to his pupil Suśruta.
history: Attributed to Suśruta, traditionally placed in ancient Vārāṇasī; scholars now hold that multiple authors contributed and that the surviving recension was reworked into the early centuries CE, with the Uttaratantra a later addition often credited to Nāgārjuna. Its principal commentary is the Nibandhasaṃgraha of Ḍalhaṇa (12th c.). It is renowned as one of the earliest systematic surgical texts.

title: Manusmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Manu)
genre: dharmaśāstra (treatise on law, duty, and social order)
date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (range 2nd c. BCE-3rd c. CE)
summary: The most influential of the Dharmaśāstras, a versified treatise in twelve chapters laying out the duties (dharma) of the varṇas and stages of life, kingship and statecraft, civil and criminal law, marriage and inheritance, purity, penance, and karma and rebirth. This record (work_id suffix _o) is a variant/version of the same Mānava-Dharmaśāstra.
history: Ascribed to the primordial lawgiver Manu but composed by an unknown author or redactor; Patrick Olivelle argues for a single organizing author and dates it to the 2nd-3rd c. CE on the basis of vocabulary and the mention of gold coins. It accreted numerous commentaries (Medhātithi, Govindarāja, Kullūka) and became the paradigmatic Hindu legal text, with an enormous and contested modern reception.

title: Manusmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Manu)
genre: dharmaśāstra (treatise on law, duty, and social order)
date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (range 2nd c. BCE-3rd c. CE)
summary: The Mānava-Dharmaśāstra, a versified treatise in twelve chapters on dharma: the duties of the varṇas and life-stages, royal governance and statecraft, the eighteen titles of law, marriage, inheritance, ritual purity, penance, and the workings of karma and rebirth. It is the single most authoritative and most cited work of the Hindu legal tradition.
history: Traditionally attributed to Manu but of unknown authorship; Olivelle's critical edition argues for composition by one gifted author or editor around the 2nd-3rd c. CE. It generated a major commentarial literature (Medhātithi, Kullūka, Govindarāja, Rāghavānanda) and remained the touchstone of dharmaśāstra; this entry duplicates work_id GSD36manu_o (a different recension/copy of the same work).

title: Manubhāṣya
author: Medhātithi
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (range 9th-11th c.)
summary: The earliest fully surviving and most learned commentary on the Manusmṛti, the Manubhāṣya glosses Manu's verses, expounds disputed topics, raises and answers objections of opponents, and applies Mīmāṃsā interpretive principles to harmonize the legal material. It is prized for its philosophical depth and breadth of citation.
history: Composed by Medhātithi, very probably a Kashmiri scholar usually dated to the 9th-10th century; the text survives incompletely and was partly restored from later sources. It became the most authoritative voice in the Manu commentarial tradition and was heavily drawn upon by later commentators and digest-writers.

title: Viṣṇusmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Viṣṇu)
date_estimate: c. 7th-10th c. CE (Olivelle: probably 700-1000 CE, Kashmir); older limits 300 BCE-1000 CE
summary: Also called the Vaiṣṇava-Dharmaśāstra or Viṣṇu-sūtra, a legal-religious text largely in prose with framing verses, cast as a dialogue in which the god Viṣṇu instructs the Earth-goddess. It covers the usual dharma topics (varṇa duties, law, penance, śrāddha) but is distinctive for its strong bhakti orientation, its prescription of daily pūjā to Viṣṇu, and its treatment of satī and pilgrimage (tīrtha).
history: Traditionally framed as revelation by Viṣṇu and built on an older Kāṭhaka Dharmasūtra core, but Patrick Olivelle argues the extant text is a late compilation made in Kashmir around 700-1000 CE, evidenced by its bhakti framing, vocabulary, references to written documents, and Vaiṣṇava iconography paralleling post-8th-century Kashmiri images. It is the only Dharmaśāstra that does not directly treat the means of knowing dharma.

title: Āpastamba-Dharmasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional (Āpastamba school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
date_estimate: c. 5th-3rd c. BCE (often regarded as the oldest extant Dharmasūtra)
summary: An early aphoristic (sūtra-style) manual of dharma forming part of the Āpastamba Kalpasūtra of the Taittirīya branch of the Black Yajurveda. It treats the conduct of the Vedic student, householder duties, rules of eating and purity, penances, inheritance, and the duties of the king, in a concrete and relatively undeveloped legal idiom.
history: Belongs to the ritual corpus of the Āpastamba śākhā; Olivelle regards it as the earliest surviving Dharmasūtra (predating Gautama) because of its archaic terminology and its silence on mixed castes. It survives with the commentary of Haradatta (Ujjvalā) and is a key source for the prehistory of Hindu law.

title: Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional (Baudhāyana school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
date_estimate: c. 500-200 BCE (Kane); composite with later additions
summary: An early Dharmasūtra of the Baudhāyana branch of the Taittirīya Yajurveda, in mixed prose and verse, dealing with the sources of dharma, the four life-stages, rules for students and householders, food and purity, penances, inheritance, the duties of women, and ascetic and ritual matters. It is notable for explicit discussion of regional customs (ācāra) and mixed castes.
history: Part of the large Baudhāyana Kalpasūtra; it is widely held to be composite, with later strata added to an early core, complicating its dating. It survives with the commentary of Govindasvāmin and is one of the four canonical Dharmasūtras edited and translated together by Olivelle.

title: Baudhāyana-Śrautasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional (Baudhāyana school of the Taittirīya Yajurveda)
date_estimate: c. 800-600 BCE (late Brāhmaṇa / early Sūtra period)
summary: A late-Vedic manual prescribing the solemn (śrauta) sacrifices of the Taittirīya school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, including the agnyādhāna, agnihotra, new- and full-moon offerings, the soma rituals, and the great fire-altar (agnicayana) constructions. It is one of the earliest Śrautasūtras and shows the first movement toward the terse sūtra style. NOTE: the project metadata flags this work_id as mislabeled, but the supplied title is the Baudhāyana Śrautasūtra, which is described here.
history: Belongs to the Baudhāyana Kalpasūtra and is dated to roughly 800-600 BCE, composed in eastern Uttar Pradesh during the late Brāhmaṇa period; it is famous in Indo-Aryan studies for a passage discussed in migration debates. It was critically edited by C. G. Kashikar. It is distinct from the (much later) Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra of the same school.

title: Gautama-Dharmasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional (Gautama; associated with the Sāmaveda)
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (often regarded as among the oldest, possibly the oldest, Dharmasūtras)
summary: The shortest and one of the earliest Dharmasūtras, written entirely in prose and traditionally attached to the Sāmaveda. It systematically treats the sources of dharma, initiation and studentship, the duties of the four varṇas and life-stages, rules of purity and food, penances, inheritance, and the law and duties of the king, in a notably orderly arrangement.
history: Ascribed to a sage Gautama; Olivelle (following some predecessors) and Lingat both rank it among the earliest extant Dharmasūtras, with Lingat regarding it as the oldest. It survives with the Mitākṣarā commentary of Haradatta and the Maskarī-bhāṣya, and is one of the four core Dharmasūtras edited by Olivelle.

title: Vāsiṣṭha-Dharmasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional (Vasiṣṭha school)
date_estimate: c. 300-100 BCE (generally the latest of the four canonical Dharmasūtras); composite
summary: A Dharmasūtra in mixed prose and verse traditionally linked to the sage Vasiṣṭha and (loosely) to the Ṛgveda, treating the sources and authority of dharma, the four life-stages, the duties of varṇas, marriage and women, purity and food rules, penances, inheritance, and royal duties. It cites earlier authorities and quotes Manu, marking its relatively later position.
history: Generally placed last among the four classical Dharmasūtras on the basis of its developed terminology and quotations; it too is composite, with later interpolations. It survives with a commentary attributed to Kṛṣṇapaṇḍita (Vidvanmodinī) and is one of the four Dharmasūtras edited and translated by Olivelle.

title: Yogavāsiṣṭha
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vālmīki; core text = Mokṣopāya, of unknown Kashmiri authorship)
tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta with Yoga and Kashmir Śaiva/Trika influences)
date_estimate: core (Mokṣopāya) c. 10th c. CE Kashmir; expanded/Vedāntized 11th-14th c.; disputed
summary: A vast philosophical work structured as Vasiṣṭha's instruction to the young prince Rāma, teaching liberation through the illusoriness of the world, the mind-made nature of reality, and the path of knowledge, illustrated by hundreds of intricate, often fantastical, frame-stories. It expounds a non-dualism close to Advaita Vedānta and is also known in a shorter recension (Laghu-Yogavāsiṣṭha).
history: Attributed to the poet Vālmīki, but the underlying text is the Kashmiri Mokṣopāya, composed (per Slaje and Hanneder) around the 10th century and subsequently reworked into an orthodox Advaita scripture with Trika overtones between the 11th and 14th centuries; the abridgment is credited to Abhinanda of Kashmir. It became a hugely popular text of practical non-dualist spirituality.

title: Tantravārttika (on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra / Śābarabhāṣya)
author: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa
date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (Kumārila fl. c. 600-660 CE)
summary: The Tantravārttika is the second of Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's three sub-commentaries on Śabara's Bhāṣya on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra (the root text named in this entry's title), covering adhyāya 1.2 through adhyāya 3. It elaborates Mīmāṃsā theory of Vedic injunction, the authority and self-validity of scripture, rules of textual interpretation, and vigorously defends ritual orthopraxy against Buddhist and other opponents.
history: Composed by Kumārila Bhaṭṭa, the great 7th-century champion of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, as part of the trio Ślokavārttika, Tantravārttika, and Ṭupṭīkā. It became a central authority of the Bhāṭṭa school and exerted wide influence on later Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra exegesis, and the broader debate with Buddhism. (The entry title pairs the root Mīmāṃsāsūtra with Kumārila's vārttika.)

title: Nyāyaprakāśa (Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa / Āpadevī)
author: Āpadeva (most likely)
date_estimate: early 17th c. CE (if Āpadeva's Āpadevī)
summary: On the most probable identification, this is the Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa (popularly called the Āpadevī), a concise and lucid manual summarizing the essential interpretive principles (nyāyas) of the Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā system from the Bhāṭṭa standpoint. It became the standard introductory text used by pandits to teach Mīmāṃsā, prized for clarity and brevity over Kumārila's more elaborate works.
history: Composed by Āpadeva, a scholar who flourished in the early 17th century. The bare title 'Nyāyaprakāśa' is somewhat ambiguous, but it is most plausibly this widely used Mīmāṃsā primer (edited and translated by Franklin Edgerton) rather than a Nyāya-school work; identification is therefore reasonably but not fully certain.

title: Nyāyamañjarī
author: Jayanta Bhaṭṭa
genre: philosophical treatise (independent commentary on the Nyāyasūtra)
date_estimate: late 9th c. CE (Jayanta fl. c. 820-900 CE, Kashmir)
summary: An encyclopedic and independent-minded exposition of Nyāya philosophy structured loosely around selected Nyāyasūtras, examining the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇas: perception, inference, comparison, testimony), the validity and authorship of the Veda, theory of meaning and the sphoṭa, the existence of God, and the refutation of rival positions (Buddhist, Mīmāṃsā, Cārvāka).
history: Composed in Kashmir in the latter ninth century by Jayanta Bhaṭṭa, an adviser to King Śaṅkaravarman (whom he names), giving an unusually secure date. Written in elegant, lively prose, it is a landmark in the historiography of Indian philosophy and a major source for ninth-century intellectual debate; Jayanta also wrote the satirical drama Āgamaḍambara.

title: Manthānabhairava Tantra
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Hindu (Kaula Tantra; Western Transmission / Paścimāmnāya, Kubjikā cult)
date_estimate: c. 9th-12th c. CE (associated with the Kubjikā corpus; cult peaked 12th c.)
summary: A large Kaula tantric scripture of the Western Transmission centered on the goddess Kubjikā ('the hunchbacked/crooked one', identified with Kuṇḍalinī) and the deity Bhairava, expounding the metaphysics, sacred geography, mantra-systems, deity hierarchies, and ritual and yogic practice of this esoteric tradition. The portion translated by Dyczkowski (Kumārikākhaṇḍa) concerns the Virgin Goddess.
history: Belongs to the Kubjikā/Western-Kaula body of scriptures related to the Kubjikāmatatantra and was transmitted largely in Nepalese manuscripts. Mark S. G. Dyczkowski spent roughly a decade editing and translating it into a 14-volume work, making this previously secretive material accessible. The Kubjikā cult flourished especially around the 12th century.

title: Tantrāloka
author: Abhinavagupta
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism; Trika / Anuttara Trika, Pratyabhijñā-informed)
date_estimate: c. 1000 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE, Kashmir)
summary: Abhinavagupta's monumental synthesis of Trika Śaivism, the 'Light on the Tantras,' comprising over 5,800 verses across 37 chapters (āhnikas). It systematizes non-dual Śaiva metaphysics, the means to liberation (the upāyas), initiation, ritual, mantra, the divine energies, and the sacred calendar, interpreting tantric practice through the philosophical categories of the Pratyabhijñā. This entry is part 1 of the work as split in the project corpus.
history: Composed in early-11th-century Kashmir by Abhinavagupta, the greatest master of the Trika; it draws together and ranks the diverse Śaiva scriptural traditions into a coherent system. Its indispensable commentary is the Viveka of Jayaratha (12th-13th c.). It is regarded as the supreme encyclopedic statement of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism.

title: Tantrāloka
author: Abhinavagupta
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism; Trika / Anuttara Trika, Pratyabhijñā-informed)
date_estimate: c. 1000 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE, Kashmir)
summary: A second portion of Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka, the comprehensive 37-chapter verse exposition of non-dual Trika Śaivism covering metaphysics, the means to liberation, initiation, ritual, mantra, and tantric philosophy. This entry corresponds to part 2 of the work as divided in the project corpus and is identical in authorship and character to part 1.
history: Same work and author as GSP30tantralu1: written in early-11th-century Kashmir by Abhinavagupta and elucidated by Jayaratha's Viveka commentary. The split into two records is an artifact of the corpus rather than a distinct text. It is the foundational encyclopedic synthesis of Kashmir Śaiva tantra.

title: Īśvarapratyabhijñā-vṛtti
author: Utpaladeva
date_estimate: c. 925 CE (Utpaladeva fl. c. 900-950 CE, Kashmir)
summary: Utpaladeva's own concise commentary (vṛtti) on his Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the verse foundation of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') philosophy. The work argues that liberation comes through the recognition that one's own self is identical with the supreme Lord (Śiva), grounding a monistic, idealist, and theistic system in a sophisticated theory of consciousness, agency, and cognition.
history: Composed by Utpaladeva, pupil of Somānanda (the school's founder), around 925 CE; Abhinavagupta reports that the kārikās and the vṛtti were written together. The kārikās with this vṛtti became the doctrinal core of the Pratyabhijñā, later expounded in Abhinavagupta's two large commentaries (the Vimarśinī and the Vivṛtivimarśinī). It is the pivotal philosophical text of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism.
